title: Is a home battery worth it without solar panels?
description: A home battery charged on cheap overnight electricity can save £350 to £550 a year without solar, but payback runs 9 to 14 years. The honest UK maths.
blurb: Charging a battery on cheap overnight electricity works without solar, but the payback maths is tighter than installers suggest.
lede: Home batteries used to be sold as an add-on for solar panels, somewhere to put your spare summer sunshine. The pitch has changed. Falling battery prices, 0% VAT on standalone installations and overnight tariffs under 7p per kWh mean installers now sell batteries to homes with no panels at all. The idea is simple: fill the battery with cheap night-rate electricity and run the house off it during the day, when grid electricity costs 26.11p per kWh on the current price cap. The idea is sound. Whether the numbers stack up for your house is a different question, and the answer depends almost entirely on three figures: what you pay for the battery, the gap between your night and day rates, and how much electricity you can actually push through it.
short_answer: A home battery can pay for itself without solar, but only just. A 10 kWh system costs roughly £4,500 to £7,000 installed, and charging it overnight at around 7p per kWh instead of buying daytime electricity at 26.11p saves a typical household £350 to £550 a year. That puts payback at about 9 to 14 years, which is similar to most battery warranties. It works best for high-use households who already have a cheap overnight tariff, usually because they drive an EV.

sections.h2: How a battery saves money with no solar to store
sections.html:
Without panels, a battery has exactly one way to earn its keep: buying electricity when it is cheap and replacing electricity you would otherwise buy when it is expensive. This is called tariff arbitrage, and we cover the mechanics in detail in our guide to charging a battery on a cheap night tariff.

The routine looks like this:

  • You switch to a time-of-use tariff with a cheap overnight window, typically 5.5p to 9p per kWh for a few hours after midnight.
  • The battery charges from the grid during that window, scheduled automatically by its app.
  • From morning until the cheap window opens again, the house draws from the battery instead of the grid.
  • Every kWh the battery supplies during the day is a kWh you did not buy at the daytime rate.

That is the whole model. There is no export income worth chasing, because export tariffs are designed for solar battery storage and most explicitly exclude or limit electricity that came from the grid in the first place. Some suppliers do offer smart tariffs that occasionally pay batteries to discharge at peak times, and grid flexibility schemes pay small sums for turning down demand on winter evenings, but treat anything beyond simple day-night arbitrage as a bonus rather than a line in your payback sum.

One thing the model does require: a genuinely cheap overnight rate. On a flat tariff you pay the same rate at 3am as at 6pm, so a battery saves you nothing at all. The tariff switch is not optional, it is the entire mechanism.

sections.h2: The maths: what the day-night spread is actually worth
sections.html:
Start with the spread. On the current price cap, daytime electricity costs 26.11p per kWh. A typical EV or smart tariff overnight rate is around 7p. That looks like a 19p saving per kWh, but batteries are not free to fill: between charging losses and inverter losses, around 10 per cent of what goes in never comes out. To deliver 1 kWh you buy about 1.1 kWh, so each kWh the battery supplies really costs you about 7.8p, for a true saving of roughly 18.3p per kWh shifted.

The second number that matters is how much you can shift. A battery only saves money on electricity your house actually consumes during expensive hours. The average UK home uses around 7.5 kWh per day in total, so a 10 kWh battery in an average home is rarely emptied. Bigger households, home workers and all-electric homes can shift far more.

Electricity shifted per dayShifted per yearAnnual savingPayback on a £5,500 system
5 kWh (smaller home)1,825 kWh£33416.5 years
8 kWh (typical family home)2,920 kWh£53510.3 years
11 kWh (large or all-electric home)4,015 kWh£7367.5 years

Saving of 18.3p per kWh shifted, based on a 26.11p per kWh daytime rate (Ofgem price cap, July to September 2026) and a 7p overnight rate, with 90 per cent round-trip efficiency. Checked June 2026.

There is a harsher way to frame the same numbers. A £5,500 battery cycled once a day for ten years delivers roughly 33,000 kWh, which works out at about 17p per kWh in hardware cost alone before you have bought any electricity. Add the 7.8p charging cost and each battery-supplied kWh costs around 25p over the system's life, against 26.11p from the grid. On mid-range hardware prices the battery barely beats simply paying the cap rate. The case only becomes comfortable when you buy the battery cheaply, shift a lot of energy, or secure an overnight rate at the bottom of the range. We work through payback scenarios in more depth in our guide to battery storage payback.

A 10 kWh lithium iron phosphate battery with a hybrid or AC-coupled inverter typically costs £4,500 to £7,000 fully installed in 2026. Smaller 5 kWh systems start around £3,000 to £4,000, but the fixed costs of the inverter, electrician and scaffolding-free wall space mean cost per kWh falls sharply as systems get bigger. The hardware itself has been getting cheaper year on year, which cuts both ways: it improves the sums, but it also means waiting a year has often been the most profitable decision a battery buyer could make.

Three practical points on the buying side:

  • VAT is currently 0% on home battery installations in Great Britain, including standalone batteries fitted without solar. The relief is scheduled to run until 31 March 2027, after which the rate is due to revert. Check the current rules on the official GOV.UK energy-saving materials VAT page before you commit, because reliefs like this change.
  • Your installer must notify your network operator. Most home batteries fall under a G98 notification (done after installation) or need G99 approval in advance for larger systems. A reputable installer handles this; if one shrugs it off, walk away.
  • Check the warranty in cycles, not just years. Most batteries carry a 10 year warranty with a throughput or cycle limit, often around 6,000 cycles or a stated total energy figure. Daily arbitrage uses roughly 365 cycles a year, comfortably inside that, but twice-daily cycling on an agile-style tariff eats the allowance twice as fast.

There are no national grants for standalone home batteries in 2026. The zero VAT rate is the only direct support, so be sceptical of any sales pitch built around a "government scheme" for batteries.

sections.h2: Who a battery genuinely works for without solar
sections.html:
The economics swing on circumstances, and a few household types sit firmly on the right side of the line:

  • EV drivers already on a cheap overnight tariff. If you charge a car at 5.5p to 9p per kWh, the tariff is already in place and the spread is already proven on your bill. A battery simply extends the cheap window to the whole house. At a 5.5p overnight rate the saving rises to about 20p per kWh shifted.
  • High-consumption households. Home workers, big families, hot tub owners and anyone using well above the 2,700 kWh per year average have the daytime demand to actually empty a battery, which is what drives payback.
  • All-electric homes. Houses with no gas, especially those with heat pumps or storage heating, push serious electricity through expensive hours in winter. These homes can justify larger batteries and shift 10 to 15 kWh a day or more.
  • Homes planning solar later. If panels are a year or two away, an AC-coupled battery bought now slots into that system later. Read up on how solar panels work and whether solar is worth it first though, because if your roof suits panels, solar plus battery in one installation is usually better value than battery first.

And the households for whom it rarely works: low users in small flats, anyone unable or unwilling to move off a flat-rate tariff, and anyone whose main motivation is backup power. Most standard battery installations shut down during a power cut for safety reasons unless you pay extra for a backup gateway or changeover arrangement, and UK power cuts are rare enough that this feature is comfort, not economics.

Five things to price in before you sign anything:

  • The day rate on time-of-use tariffs is often above the cap. Cheap nights are part-funded by daytime rates a few pence higher than the 26.11p cap rate. If your battery covers nearly all daytime use this barely matters, but if it runs flat by mid-afternoon you buy the remainder at the dearer rate, which quietly erodes the saving.
  • Round-trip losses are real. Budget 10 to 15 per cent of every charge lost as heat. Quoted savings that multiply the full day-night spread by battery capacity are ignoring physics.
  • Capacity fades. Most warranties guarantee 70 to 80 per cent of original capacity at year ten. Your year-nine savings will be noticeably smaller than your year-one savings, so a quoted payback that lands at year ten is really landing later.
  • The spread is not guaranteed for a decade. Today's arbitrage case rests on a 19p gap between night and day rates. Cheap overnight windows exist because demand is low and wind output is often high overnight, and that logic looks durable, but tariffs are commercial products and the cap rate moves every three months. Nobody can promise the 2026 spread in 2033.
  • Usable capacity is less than the headline. Some systems reserve a slice of capacity to protect the cells. A "10 kWh" battery may offer 9 to 9.5 kWh usable. Quote your sums on the usable figure.

None of these kill the case. Together they explain why honest payback estimates run several years longer than the ones on installers' homepages.

A home battery without solar is no longer a daft idea, but in 2026 it is a marginal investment for the average household and a decent one for the right household. If you already have a cheap EV tariff, use well above average electricity, and can buy a 10 kWh-class system at the lower end of the £4,500 to £7,000 range while the 0% VAT rate lasts, you can reasonably expect £450 to £700 a year back and a payback inside the warranty period. If you are an average user on a flat tariff hoping the battery will somehow save money on its own, it will not.

Run your own numbers before talking to any installer: take your annual daytime consumption in kWh, multiply by the gap between a real overnight rate you qualify for and your current unit rate, knock off 10 per cent for losses, and divide the installed price by the result. If the answer is under ten years, get quotes. If it is over fifteen, put the money into insulation or wait for prices to fall further. And if your roof faces anywhere near south, price up solar with battery storage as a package before committing to a battery alone, because the panels turn a marginal battery into a well-fed one.

faq.q: Can I add solar panels to a battery later?
faq.a: Usually, yes. An AC-coupled battery works independently of any solar inverter, so panels can be added later with minimal rework. If you choose a hybrid inverter system, tell the installer solar is planned so they size and configure it accordingly. If solar is likely within a couple of years, getting one combined quote now is often cheaper than two separate installations.

faq.q: Will a home battery keep my lights on during a power cut?
faq.a: Not by default. Standard grid-tied battery systems shut down in a power cut for the safety of engineers working on the lines. Backup capability needs extra hardware, often called a gateway or EPS, which adds several hundred pounds or more and usually only powers selected circuits. If backup is your main motivation, specify it explicitly when getting quotes.

faq.q: How long does a home battery last?
faq.a: Most lithium iron phosphate home batteries carry a 10 year warranty, typically guaranteeing around 70 to 80 per cent of original capacity at the end of it, with a cycle or throughput limit attached. Cycled once a day for arbitrage, a battery uses about 365 cycles a year, so a 6,000 cycle rating comfortably outlasts the warranty. Realistic working life is 10 to 15 years.

faq.q: Do I need an electric car to get a cheap overnight tariff?
faq.a: For the very cheapest rates, usually yes: the 5.5p to 7p tariffs are mostly EV tariffs that require a compatible car or charger. Without an EV you can still get Economy 7 from any supplier, with night rates commonly in the 9p to 14p range, and some suppliers offer smart tariffs aimed specifically at battery owners. The spread is smaller, so the battery saving is smaller too.

faq.q: Is there a grant for home batteries in the UK?
faq.a: There is no national grant for standalone home batteries in 2026. The main support is the 0% VAT rate on battery installations in Great Britain, scheduled to last until 31 March 2027. A handful of local schemes and supplier offers come and go, so check GOV.UK and your council's energy pages for current rules before buying.
